input.description: Inspect docker networks and gh-sr containers

content.text:
The host's default Docker bridge is confirmed as `172.17.0.0/16` (gateway `172.17.0.1`). This is the smoking gun. Let me check everywhere `172.17.0.1` is referenced and review the network test before deciding the fix.

[REDACTED]

Let me read `environment.go` (doctor uses a hardcoded `@172.17.0.1`) and the network test to understand all coupling.

[REDACTED]

Let me read the relevant test sections that assert these IPs.

[REDACTED]

I've confirmed the root cause and the design constraints. Let me record the plan and start fixing.

## Root cause

The agentic refactor (commit `552336d`) added a **baked** `daemon.json` that hardcodes the inner Docker bridge to:

```1:4:internal/runner/agentic-runner-image/daemon.json
{
  "bip": "172.17.0.1/24",
  "dns": ["172.17.0.1", "8.8.8.8"]
}
```

The outer runner container is created with no `--network` (so it lands on the host's **default Docker bridge**, which on your host is confirmed to be `172.17.0.0/16`, gateway `172.17.0.1`). So inside the runner container:

- `eth0` = `172.17.0.x/16`, **default route via `172.17.0.1`**
- inner `docker0` = **`172.17.0.1/24`** — the *same* IP as the container's default gateway, and an overlapping subnet.

The runner container's default-gateway IP is now also a local interface (the inner bridge), and the `172.17.0.0/24` route points at `docker0` instead of out `eth0`. Outbound traffic gets black-holed/misrouted into the inner bridge → connections crawl/time out, while the host network itself is fine. **Before the refactor there was no `bip`, so Docker's IPAM auto-avoided the `172.17` conflict and put `docker0` on `172.18.x`** — which is exactly why it worked before and broke after.

A guard test (`TestAgenticRunnerEntrypointStartsDockerdOnce`) forbids runtime `daemon.json` rewrites, so the fix must stay baked + single-dockerd-start. The fix: bake the inner bridge onto a subnet that can't collide with Docker's default pools (a `10.x` net), instead of `172.17`.

[REDACTED]

todos.content: Change baked inner bridge subnet from 172.17.0.1/24 to non-colliding 10.200.0.1/24 (daemon.json, dnsmasq conf)
Update entrypoint/docker-wrapper comments and doctor probe (environment.go) to the new gateway
Update tests asserting the old IP (container_test.go, environment_test.go)
Build + run go tests to verify

content.text: Now the fixes. First, the baked configs:

input.contents:
# gh-sr inner-container DNS (baked into the container runner image).
#
# The inner dockerd's default bridge is pinned to 10.200.0.1/24 (see daemon.json),
# so the bridge gateway IP is deterministic. dnsmasq listens on that gateway and:
#   * answers host.docker.internal with the bridge gateway IP so AWF agent /
#     bridge containers reach the MCP gateway (which gh-aw runs on the runner
#     container's host network), and
#   * forwards everything else to upstream resolvers (plus the runner container's
#     own /etc/resolv.conf) so model-provider / GitHub APIs resolve.
#
# Why 10.200.0.1 and NOT 172.17.0.1: the outer runner container itself sits on the
# host's default Docker bridge (172.17.0.0/16, gateway 172.17.0.1). Pinning the inner
# bridge to 172.17.0.x would duplicate the container's own default-gateway IP and
# overlap its eth0 subnet, silently black-holing the container's outbound traffic into
# the inner bridge (host network fine, but every connection inside the runner crawls /
# times out). 10.200.0.0/24 is outside Docker's default address pools (172.16/12 and
# 192.168/16) and the default bridge, so it cannot collide with the outer container's
# network or any auto-allocated inner network.
#
# Baking this (instead of detecting docker0 at runtime and restarting dockerd)
# lets the inner dockerd start exactly once with a stable configuration.
address=/host.docker.internal/10.200.0.1
listen-address=10.200.0.1
bind-dynamic
server=8.8.8.8
server=1.1.1.1

content.text: Confirmed — the `autostart` (launchd) failures are pre-existing on stock `main` and unrelated to this change (they fail because launchd doesn't exist on this Linux/WSL2 box). All runner/agentic/config/doctor tests pass with my fix.

Yes — this is directly caused by the agentic profile refactor, and I found and fixed it.

## What was wrong

The refactor (commit `552336d`) introduced a **baked** inner-Docker `daemon.json` that hardcodes the inner bridge:

```
"bip": "172.17.0.1/24",
"dns": ["172.17.0.1", "8.8.8.8"]
```

But the **outer runner container** is created with no `--network`, so it lands on the host's **default Docker bridge** — which on your host is `172.17.0.0/16`, gateway `172.17.0.1` (I confirmed this with `docker network inspect bridge`). So inside the runner container you get a subnet collision:

| interface | address | role |
|---|---|---|
| `eth0` (outer bridge) | `172.17.0.x/16`, default route **via `172.17.0.1`** | path to host + internet |
| inner `docker0` (baked `bip`) | **`172.17.0.1/24`** | inner bridge — *same IP as the default gateway* |

The container's own default-gateway IP (`172.17.0.1`) becomes a local interface, and the `172.17.0.0/24` route points at `docker0` instead of out `eth0`. Outbound traffic gets misrouted/black-holed into the inner bridge, so every connection inside the workflow crawls or times out — while the host's network is completely fine. That's exactly your symptom.

**Why it worked before the refactor:** the old code didn't set `bip`, so Docker's IPAM auto-detected the `172.17` conflict and put `docker0` on `172.18.x`. Hardcoding `bip` bypassed that conflict avoidance.

## The fix

A guard test (`TestAgenticRunnerEntrypointStartsDockerdOnce`) deliberately forbids runtime `daemon.json` rewrites, so I kept the baked/single-dockerd-start design and just moved the inner bridge to a subnet that **cannot** collide with Docker's default pools:

- `daemon.json` → `"bip": "10.200.0.1/24"`, `"dns": ["10.200.0.1", "8.8.8.8"]`
- `dnsmasq-gh-sr.conf` → `address`/`listen-address` = `10.200.0.1`
- Updated the doctor readiness probe (`environment.go`), entrypoint/wrapper comments, and docs
- Updated tests, and added regression assertions that the inner bridge directives never overlap `172.17.0.0/16`

`10.200.0.0/24` is outside Docker's default address pools (`172.16/12` and `192.168/16`) and the default bridge, so it can't collide with the outer container's network or any auto-allocated inner network. The gateway IP is opaque to gh-aw (`host.docker.internal` just resolves to it), and the AWF service-routing bypass still treats it as a LOCAL address, so nothing else changes behaviorally.

## To apply it

The config is baked into the runner image, so rebuild the affected runner(s):

```bash
gh sr rebuild <runner-name>
```

All `runner`, `agentic`, `config`, and `doctor` tests pass. (The two `internal/autostart` launchd test failures are pre-existing on `main` and unrelated — they fail because launchd doesn't exist on this Linux/WSL2 box.)

One caveat worth flagging: if any host ever directly attaches a runner container to a `10.200.0.0/24` (or broad `10.0.0.0/8`) network, you'd hit the same class of collision again. If you want, I can additionally add a small pre-`dockerd` collision check in `entrypoint.sh` that picks a free subnet — but that would require relaxing the "no runtime daemon.json write" guard test, so I left the design intent intact for now.

which collides with the host's default Docker bridge (172.17.0.0/16) that

container's default-gateway IP and captured its eth0 route, black-holing
all outbound traffic into the inner bridge — connections inside agentic

Pin the inner bridge to 10.200.0.1/24 instead (outside Docker's default
address pools and the default bridge), keeping the baked single-dockerd

docs, and adds regression assertions that the inner bridge never overlaps
172.17.0.0/16.
EOF

<timestamp>Sunday, May 31, 2026, 8:08 PM (UTC+8)</timestamp>
<user_query>
Let's add the collision check. And carefully document it.
</user_query>

I'll add a runtime collision check in `entrypoint.sh` that picks a free subnet before the single `dockerd` start, document it carefully, and update the guard test that currently forbids any runtime `daemon.json` write (it must still forbid the dangerous *restart* dance, while allowing a pre-start write). Let me first read that guard test in full.

[REDACTED]

Let me check the image has the tools the check needs (`ip`, `sed`, `grep`, `awk`) and re-read the exact entrypoint section where I'll insert the block.

[REDACTED]
`iproute2` (`ip`) is present; `sed`/`grep`/`awk` come with the base image. Now I'll insert the collision-avoidance subsection before the `dockerd` start.

input.old_string:
# ── 2. Inner dockerd (single start; DNS baked into /etc/docker/daemon.json) ─────
echo "[entrypoint] starting dockerd..."
dockerd \
    --data-root="${DOCKER_DATA_ROOT}" \
    --host=unix:///var/run/docker.sock \
    --log-level=warn \
    &>/runner-state/dockerd.log &
input.new_string:
# ── 2. Inner-bridge subnet collision avoidance (pre-start; no restart) ──────────
# The image bakes a deterministic inner-bridge subnet into /etc/docker/daemon.json
# (10.200.0.1/24) and the dnsmasq config. 10.200.0.0/24 is chosen because it sits
# OUTSIDE Docker's default address pools (172.16.0.0/12 and 192.168.0.0/16), so it
# does not normally collide with anything Docker auto-allocates — including the host
# default bridge 172.17.0.0/16 that the outer runner container's eth0 usually sits on.
#
# WHY THIS CHECK EXISTS (defence in depth):
#   The *outer* runner container's networks are decided by whoever runs the image
#   (the host's Docker / orchestrator), NOT by us. A custom host could attach the
#   runner container to an arbitrary subnet — conceivably even 10.200.0.0/24, or a
#   broad 10.0.0.0/8. If the baked inner-bridge subnet overlapped one of the runner
#   container's OWN interfaces, the inner docker0 would duplicate a directly-attached
#   or gateway IP and capture that route. Outbound traffic would then be black-holed
#   into the inner bridge: the host network stays fine, but every connection made
#   inside the runner (agent → model provider, git, package installs) crawls or times
#   out. This is exactly the failure that a hardcoded 172.17.0.1 bip caused, and the
#   reason we no longer pin to the default-bridge subnet.
#
#   Docker's own IPAM auto-avoids these conflicts when `bip` is unset, but we pin
#   `bip` for a deterministic dnsmasq listen address — which bypasses that safety
#   net. This block re-adds it: it validates the baked gateway against the container's
#   CURRENT interfaces and, only on conflict, rewrites daemon.json + the dnsmasq
#   config to the first collision-free candidate.
#
# CRITICAL ORDERING: this runs BEFORE the single dockerd start below. We never write
# daemon.json after dockerd is up and we never restart dockerd — the daemon reads the
# final config on its one and only start. (The old "write daemon.json then kill +
# restart dockerd" dance was a major instability source; do not reintroduce it.)
DEFAULT_BRIDGE_GW="10.200.0.1"
# Candidates are tried in order; all are /24s outside the host's default bridge.
BRIDGE_CANDIDATES=(10.200.0.1 10.201.0.1 10.210.0.1 192.168.221.1 172.28.0.1 172.20.0.1)

# subnet_is_free <gateway-ip>: true when the IP must be forwarded via a gateway
# rather than being inside a subnet directly attached to this container. `ip route
# get` returns an on-link route ("dev <if>", no "via") for an address inside a local
# subnet or equal to a directly-attached gateway, and a "via <gw>" route otherwise.
# docker0 does not exist yet (dockerd has not started), so this only tests against the
# runner container's pre-existing interfaces (eth0, lo, ...).
subnet_is_free() {
    ip -4 route get "$1" 2>/dev/null | head -n1 | grep -q ' via '
}

BRIDGE_GW=""
for _cand in "${BRIDGE_CANDIDATES[@]}"; do
    if subnet_is_free "${_cand}"; then
        BRIDGE_GW="${_cand}"
        break
    fi
done
# Last resort: if every candidate looked busy (or `ip route` is unavailable), keep the
# documented default rather than failing the whole runner.
BRIDGE_GW="${BRIDGE_GW:-$DEFAULT_BRIDGE_GW}"

if [ "${BRIDGE_GW}" != "${DEFAULT_BRIDGE_GW}" ]; then
    echo "[entrypoint] WARNING: baked inner-bridge gateway ${DEFAULT_BRIDGE_GW} overlaps an existing interface; using ${BRIDGE_GW} instead"
    # One-shot rewrite, strictly before the dockerd start below (no restart).
    cat > /etc/docker/daemon.json <<EOF
{
  "bip": "${BRIDGE_GW}/24",
  "dns": ["${BRIDGE_GW}", "8.8.8.8"]
}
EOF
    # Update only the gateway-bearing directives; the upstream `server=` lines stay.
    sed -i \
        -e "s#^address=/host.docker.internal/.*#address=/host.docker.internal/${BRIDGE_GW}#" \
        -e "s#^listen-address=.*#listen-address=${BRIDGE_GW}#" \
        /etc/dnsmasq.d/gh-sr.conf
else
    echo "[entrypoint] inner-bridge gateway ${BRIDGE_GW} (baked default; no interface conflict detected)"
fi

# ── 2b. Inner dockerd (single start; DNS baked into /etc/docker/daemon.json) ────
echo "[entrypoint] starting dockerd..."
dockerd \
    --data-root="${DOCKER_DATA_ROOT}" \
    --host=unix:///var/run/docker.sock \
    --log-level=warn \
    &>/runner-state/dockerd.log &

content.text:
Let me quickly verify the detection logic and the dnsmasq `sed` rewrite behave correctly with a standalone simulation:

[REDACTED]

cd /tmp && cat > /tmp/test_collision.sh <<'SCRIPT'
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
subnet_is_free() { ip -4 route get "$1" 2>/dev/null | head -n1 | grep -q ' via '; }
echo "--- route get samples (this WSL box) ---"
for ip in 10.200.0.1 10.201.0.1 172.17.0.1 8.8.8.8; do
  r=$(ip -4 route get "$ip" 2>/dev/null | head -n1)
  if subnet_is_free "$ip"; then echo "  $ip -> FREE   ($r)"; else echo "  $ip -> in-use ($r)"; fi
done

echo "--- sed rewrite of dnsmasq conf ---"
cp /home/an-lee/projects/gh-sr/internal/runner/agentic-runner-image/dnsmasq-gh-sr.conf /tmp/d.conf
BRIDGE_GW=10.201.0.1
sed -i \
  -e "s#^address=/host.docker.internal/.*#address=/host.docker.internal/${BRIDGE_GW}#" \
  -e "s#^listen-address=.*#listen-address=${BRIDGE_GW}#" \
  /tmp/d.conf
grep -E '^(address=|listen-address=|server=)' /tmp/d.conf
SCRIPT
bash /tmp/test_collision.sh; rm -f /tmp/test_collision.sh /tmp/d.conf

Detection and rewrite both behave correctly (note `172.17.0.1` is flagged in-use, `10.200.x` is free). Now let me add a short note to the user-facing guide. Let me see the context around my earlier doc edit:

[REDACTED]

input.old_string: - **Docker CE** (the inner `dockerd` for DinD) with a **baked daemon config** that pins the inner default bridge gateway to `10.200.0.1` and points container DNS at a bundled **dnsmasq**, so `host.docker.internal` resolves deterministically for inner containers. The inner `dockerd` starts exactly once — there is no runtime DNS rewrite or daemon restart. (`10.200.0.1` is deliberately outside the host's default Docker bridge subnet `172.17.0.0/16` so the inner bridge cannot collide with the outer runner container's own network.)

input.description: Find note styling and available shortcodes

content.text: The docs use plain markdown blockquotes (`>`) for notes, not custom shortcodes. Let me replace my invented `callout` with a blockquote:

input.new_string:
> **Why not the default `172.17.0.0/16`?** The outer runner container itself normally sits on the host's **default Docker bridge** `172.17.0.0/16` (gateway `172.17.0.1`). If the inner bridge were pinned to a `172.17.x` address it would duplicate the container's own default-gateway IP and capture that route, **black-holing all outbound traffic into the inner bridge** — the host network stays fine, but every connection made *inside* the runner (agent → model provider, `git`, package installs) crawls or times out. `10.200.0.0/24` avoids this because it lies outside Docker's default address pools (`172.16.0.0/12` and `192.168.0.0/16`).
  >
  > As **defence in depth**, `entrypoint.sh` validates the baked gateway against the runner container's *actual* interfaces just before the single `dockerd` start. If a custom host happens to attach the runner container to a subnet that overlaps `10.200.0.0/24`, the entrypoint automatically falls back to the next free candidate (`10.201.0.1`, `10.210.0.1`, …) and rewrites `daemon.json` + the dnsmasq config **once, before** `dockerd` starts (still a single start, no restart). A `WARNING: baked inner-bridge gateway … overlaps an existing interface` line in `docker logs <runner-container>` indicates the fallback was used.

content.text:
Done. The collision check is added, verified, and carefully documented. The changes are **uncommitted** (you didn't ask to commit this round — let me know if you'd like me to).

## What I added

**1. Runtime collision-avoidance in `entrypoint.sh`** (defence in depth), running *before* the single `dockerd` start:

- Tries candidate `/24` gateways in order: `10.200.0.1` (baked default), then `10.201.0.1`, `10.210.0.1`, `192.168.221.1`, `172.28.0.1`, `172.20.0.1`.
- `subnet_is_free()` uses `ip -4 route get <ip>` — an address that routes `via <gateway>` is free; one with an on-link `dev <if>` route (or `local`) is inside one of the runner container's own subnets and is skipped. (At this point `docker0` doesn't exist yet, so it only tests the container's pre-existing interfaces.)
- On conflict it rewrites `daemon.json` and the dnsmasq directives **once, before** `dockerd` starts — no post-start rewrite, no restart. Logs a clear `WARNING: baked inner-bridge gateway … overlaps an existing interface` line.
- Falls back to the documented default if every candidate looks busy.

**2. Doctor probe (`environment.go`)** now derives the live `docker0` gateway (falling back to `10.200.0.1`), so the readiness check stays correct even when the fallback picks a different subnet.

**3. Guard test (`container_test.go`)** updated: instead of forbidding any runtime `daemon.json` write, it now enforces the real invariant — `dockerd` is started exactly once, and any `daemon.json` write must occur *before* that single start (no restart dance).

**4. Documentation:** heavy inline comments in `entrypoint.sh` explaining the why/ordering, plus a user-facing blockquote in `agentic-workflows.md` ("Why not the default `172.17.0.0/16`?") describing the failure mode and the fallback behaviour.

## Verification

- I simulated `subnet_is_free` on this box: `172.17.0.1` → in-use (correctly rejected), `10.200.0.1`/`10.201.0.1` → free; and confirmed the `sed` rewrite preserves the `server=` lines.
- `bash -n` clean, `go build` clean, `gofmt`/`go vet` clean, and `runner` / `agentic` / `doctor` / `config` tests pass.
